questions.question: Explain the difference between a LinearLayout and a RelativeLayout in Android. When would you choose to use one over the other?
keyPoints: LinearLayout arranges its children in a single row (horizontal) or column (vertical), one after the other.
RelativeLayout positions its children relative to each other or to the parent container.
Use LinearLayout for simple, sequential arrangements; use RelativeLayout for more complex, view-relative positioning.

questions.question: Describe the purpose and key components of the Android View Binding mechanism. What are its main advantages over the traditional findViewById() method?
keyPoints: View Binding generates a binding class for each XML layout file, providing direct, type-safe references to all views with an ID.
Key components include the generated binding class and its methods like inflate() and getRoot().
Advantages: Eliminates the risk of ClassCastException and null pointer exceptions from invalid IDs, is faster than findViewById, and results in cleaner code.

questions.question: A developer needs to create a settings screen with various input types. Outline the process of designing this UI using modern Android development practices.
keyPoints: Use a ScrollView with a vertical LinearLayout or ConstraintLayout as the root.
Implement components: TextInputLayout/EditText for text, SwitchCompat for toggles, AppCompatSpinner for drop-down, and SeekBar for the slider.
Utilize the Preferences API (AndroidX Preference Library) to simplify the creation of a standardized settings screen.
Use data binding or view binding to reference views and attach listeners.
